chunk_summary: This section discusses transitioning from diet counseling to acute malnutrition protocols for wasted children, focusing on vegetarian and regional feeding patterns, with guidance from WHO and national guidelines.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of vegetarian and regional feeding patterns in addressing acute malnutrition in children.
Learn the operational guidelines for facility-based management of severe acute malnutrition (SAM).
Identify WHO-recommended child growth standards and their application in malnutrition protocols.
Explore complementary feeding practices as per IAP guidelines.
key_facts: Severe acute malnutrition (SAM) affects children with weight-for-height Z-scores below -3 SD of the WHO Child Growth Standards.
Facility-based management of SAM includes therapeutic feeding and medical care as per NHM guidelines.
WHO recommends energy-dense therapeutic foods with 175-200 kcal/kg/day for children with SAM.
Vegetarian diets can be adapted to meet nutritional needs in regions with specific dietary practices.
Complementary feeding should begin at 6 months of age, as per IAP guidelines, to prevent malnutrition.
WHO Child Growth Standards provide benchmarks for assessing malnutrition in children under 5 years.
reference_quote: "Facility-based management of children with severe acute malnutrition (SAM) includes therapeutic feeding and medical care as outlined in the operational guidelines." (Source: https://nhm.gov.in/images/pdf/programmes/childhealth/guidelines/operational_guidelines_on_fbmc_with_sam.pdf)
